content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcnl PnacrrcEs CouutssloN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 11,2004
William D. McMinn
Port of San Diego
Post Office Box 120488
San Diego, CA 921 12-0488
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our f ile No.I-04-042
Dear Mr. McMinn:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of the employees of the San
Diego Unified Port District ("Dishict") for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the'Act').r Since you are requesting advice onbehalf ofDistrict
employees you have not identified, and have not provided specific facts regarding each
affected employee, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to
regulation 18329(c).
Re:
I
QUESTTONS
Holland America Line ("Holland') provided District employees with a
contract number to use to receive a discount on two specific cruises.
Employees were free to provide this conhact number to friends and family to
receive the same discount. If an employee's family and friends used the
discount, was the value of the discount received by them a gift to the
employee?
If a Dishict employee's friend or family mernber provided a third party with
the contract number, and the third party received the discount, did the
employee receive a gift?
2.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 cal. code of Regs. Section
1 8329(c)(3).)(Copy enclosed.)


File No. I-04-042
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
If an employee is listed in the Diskict's conflict of interest code and is
required to report income from Holland on his or her statement of economic
interests, or is required to file a statement of economic interests under section
872a0, and provided the discount information to a friend or family merrber,
the employee is considered to have received a gift in the amount of the
discount. The friend or family member need not have used the information
nor received the discount for the employee to have received the gift.
If the employee had no control over the provision of the discount information
to a third pffity, the employee did not receive a gft when the third ptray
received the information, or the discount was received.
T.ACTS
The District is govemed by a board ofport commissioners and has jurisdiction
over tidelands surrounding the San Diego Bay, submergd lands in the Bay, and any
other lands acquired by the District, which are held in trust for the benefit of the people of
the State of California for the purposes of commerce, navigation, fisheries, and
recreation, as codified in the san Diego unified Port District Act.
You are a deputy port attomey and are requesting advice on behalf of the District.
The District received a flier from Holland, which offered a "special for San Diego port
Employees, Friends and Family." fhe special allowed Dstrict employees, and their
friends and family, to receive a discount in the price of a crtrise for the weeks of January
3l-February 7,2004, and February 7-l4,zo0/'. A District employee, and friends and
family of the District employee, could take advantage of the discount by using a contract
number listed on the flier. Holland has confirmed that the discount was not Uiing offered
to the public in the normal course ofbusiness. As a result, Dishict employees may have
considered taking advantage of this offer, and also may have considered passing the
contract number on to their friends and families, who in turn mayhavepassed the
contract number on to others, and so on.
AIYALYSIS
Public officials, other than those covered by section 87200,3 who make,
participate in making or use their official position to influence govemmental decisions,
must disclose certain economic interests as required by their respective agency's conflict
of interest code. (Sections 87300, 573U.) Referred to as designated emfloyees, they
report the receipt of gifts valued at $50 or more in the aggregatein a calendar yeax from a
single source if the source is a fipe of income required to be disclosed under the conflict
of interest code for the agency. (Sections 82030; 87302.) In addition, a designated
employee may not receive a gift of more than $340 from a reportable source in a calendar
year. (Section 89503; regulation 18940.2.) A g1ft of $340 from a single reportable
source may cause a conflict of interest for the employee. (Sections 87100; S7103(e).)
'Prblic officials who are covered by section 872}O,city council members, boards ofsupervisors,
for example, disclose all sources of gifts of $50 or more unless a statutory exception applies.
I
2


File No. I-04-042
Page No. 3
Since you do not ask whether the discount offered was a gifta to a District
employee, we essume that your own analysis provides that the discount wr6 a gift to
employees who took advantage of it. In addition, since the dates for the cruises have
passed, we are addressing only the future obligations of employees under the District's
conflict of interest code; the Commission does not provide advice relating to past
conduct. (Regulation I 8329(b)(8XA).)
Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as:
". . .[AJny payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipien! to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the
rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of
the public without regard to official status."
Thus, a discount is a gift, unless the discount is made in the regular course of
business to members of thepublic without regard to official status.
The Act also provides for exceptions to the definition of a gift. Regulation
18946.1(a) states, in relevant part, that "[aJ pass or ticket has no value unless it is
ultimately used or transferred to another person."
Also, regulation 189a1(a)(1) and (3) state:
"(a) ... a gft is 'received' or 'accepted' when the recipient knows that he
or she has either actual possession of the gift ortakes any action
exercising direction or control over the gift.
"(l) In the case of a rebate or discount ... the gift is .received' or
'accepted' when the recipient knows that the rebate or discount is not
made in the regular course of business to members of the public
without regard to official status.
lt.. .It
"(3) Turning a grft over to another person does not negate receipt or
acceptance of a gift."
Thus, if the employee did not use the discount,-the ernployee did not receive a reportable
gift. Nevertheless, if the employee transferreds the offer to someone else, such as a friend
or family member, the ernployee did receive a reportable gift from Holland in the amount
a For example, regulation 1 8 9a 1 (a)( I ) provides that in the case of a rebate or discount, the gift is
o'received" or "accepted" when the recipient knows that the rebate or discount is not made in the regular
course of business to members of the public without regard to official status.
5 The statutory definition of gift refers to a "payment." (Section 52028(a).) The Act's definition
of"paymenf is verybroad, and includes "a ... transfer... of ... anything else ofvalue, whethertangible or
intangible." (Section 820M.) Thus, an employee's hansfer of the discount to another person was a
"pa5rment," within the meaning of the gift definition.


File No. I-04-042
Page No. 4
of the fair market value of the discount, even if the person to whom the transfer was made
ultimately did not receive a discount.
However, if an employee hansferred the offer to a friend or family member, and
the friend or family mernber in turn transferred it to a third part5 if the employee did not
exercise direction or control over the transfer to the third party, the employee did not
receive an additional gift.
Regulation 18943 provides that "[a] gift is neither accepted nor received if it is
returned, donated, or reimbursed...." In the sifuation in which an employee used the
discount, or transferred the discount to anotherindividual, the employee would not be
required to report the receipt of a gift, or would be required to report a reduced amount,
if:
"The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or acceptancq reimburses the
donor, or the donor's agent or intermediary, for all or a portion of the gift.
In such event the value of the gift is reducd by the amount of the
reimbursement, and the amount of any grft or activity expense which
must be disclosed is reduced by the amount ofthe reimburseme,nt."
(Regulation I 89a3(a)(a).)
As an example, if the employee accepted the cruise for $199, and the fairmarket
value of the cruise was, in fact, $499, the employee would be requird to report the
receipt of a gift of $300. However, if, within 30 days ofreceipt or acceptance of the gift,
the employee paid Holland the entire fair market value for the cruisg no grft was received
under the Act.
Please be advised that Article XII, Section 7, of the California Constitution states
that *[a] transportation company may not grant free passes or discounts to anyone
holding an office in this state; and the acceptance of a pa.ss or discount by a public
officer, other than a Public Utilities Commissioner, shall work a forfeiture of that office.,,
Further information on the applicability ofthis provision to your facts and other laws
should be obtained from the Attorney General's office.
If you have any other questions regarding this matt€r, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Technical Assistance Division
Enclosure
KSMjg
I:\Adviceltrs\O4-042
04u.
By:

formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 cal. code of Regs. Section
